Yeah, so, mean, again, know Tucker shared a bunch already, but, like, in general, just, like, at as high level as possible, like, as a company, what we're trying to do is actually trying to figure out, like, how knowledge work should be done in this, like, kind of AI phase of, like, work now.

Because what we see is, like, developers just having just, like, they're living in, like, a totally different world, like, agents are running kind of in the background, they connect with every tool, because they already talk to each other, and then, like, marketers are still kind of in the same Google Docs kind of rewriting and, you know, repetitively doing things that will probably get replaced with AI very soon.
And our initial kind of bet into that journey is, like, building this agency called GrowthX, where the goal really was just to figure out ourselves, like.
What it means to work like AI native.
And, you know, like I think sales is like a reasonable suite of tools now, Apollo being one of them in clay.
But like marketing is still kind of in this weird spot.
And if you think about what marketing is, it's like words on a page, you know, at the end of the day.
And like, there's like no reason LLMs can't be like a part of that process.
We created this agency.
What we are is like, you know, kind of a partner for most of our clients where we own the content part of their growth kind of org.
So a lot of it's SEO.
Now it's AEO.
You know, sometimes we go into like socials and kind of case studies and, you know, documentation even for some clients.

But our approach is essentially kind of instead of, you know, hiring this like roster of people, we build these like systems and machines of like AI agents that do things from like research to drafting articles.
And these systems also have.
Like real humans that like, you know, some of them are experts in like copywriting and reviewing.
Some of them are experts in like the domain itself.
So like maybe a concrete example for Webflow, they, you know, they want more developers to kind of use their products.
So they've been trying to like build this huge resource for them where essentially anything you wanted to like build on top of Webflow, there's like a doc that you could read for it.
And we've built kind of this machine that's like essentially like building that part of their site up.
So like every week, you know, we publish like 30 to 40 articles there.
And then once we're done, we go back and it's almost like you're treating content as a product.
And that's working, you know, exceptionally well.
It's not as scalable as, you know, we want, but like that is the problem we're trying to figure out.
I think it went from like essentially zero to like 12 or 13 million in revenue in the last year.
And yeah, so that's kind of what.
Like the agency bet.
then for just our marketing in general, like what's been tremendously helpful for us is just giving value to people.

Like we're not selling a tool for other people to use and do the work.

So our own marketing for the longest time has just been to kind of evangelize that, like do these workshops.

And then, you know, then becoming customers anyway.
So like.

But they just don't know how to like do that, you know, like even in this bubble in like the Bay Area, like most of the marketers we talk to like have no real idea how to use AI.

And that's like in this bubble, you know, like you go out to the rest of the world, the rest of the US, like there's just this huge gap in like, you know, people having kind of a place to like support them on that journey.
So I guess the idea was to like create that community.
Still very like kind of content heavy, you know, for the most part of the community is just like we do a workshop, you know, 500 to 1,000 people come to it.
They all ask for the recording and then I just like send them the recording in the community.
And then they're like, oh, cool.
There's other stuff here.
And then they pay for it.
I think right now we have 70 paying members.
And yeah, so that's kind of like the part where I'm pretty confident there's something there to build out, but just not sure exactly how to do that.
And we're kind of looking for somebody to kind of own that part of the business.

Like it really should play in how you think about content marketing, demand generation, all of that.

And that's kind of like the gap that I've seen when, you know, I've joined companies who are consulted for, you know, B2B community building, where a lot of times like they have this community that's not really connected with the rest of the way they market.

So yeah, I've been in the community space for, I would say like seven-ish years now.
And I think like, I don't, I don't really brand myself as a B2B community marketer.
I think I'm actually in a mix of community, customer marketing, and demand gen, because I think all of these pieces play together in how you think about community.
And yeah, like right now, I lead the Apollo community.

And then that includes like our own community, which we hosted on Slack just to make it easier.
We also have, you know, in-person events, online workshops, similar to what you guys do.
We don't really have any paid play kind of pieces just yet in the community, but that is something that the execs have been thinking of doing, you know, for some of the more like VIP customers.
But one thing that I think is pretty interesting is that we've also started to do a lot more of like how to use these tech tools to do X and Y.
So like more of these how-to content, and that's starting with obviously our own product, but I think we're going to start like bringing in other vendors as well.
So there's that flywheel of like working with partners, agencies.
So yeah, some quick context on that.

I mean, like I think of community almost like an extension of like a product, right?
It's kind of like you got to like build these retention loops, engagement loops, and like discovery loops.

So I feel like it's like a two-prone problem where I feel like there needs to be some sort of like maybe a compelling reason for people to discover the community, whether it's right now, it's through the events, but then maybe through content and otherwise.
Because I think you guys have some great content through the masterclasses and your blog, right?
But having like a better tie into that to let people know like this is something that you can get, that's extra value add.
And then also I think there should be.
It's some sort of like, maybe more of an organic funnel as well, where, you know, like through the testimonies you have in the community, right?
Like having people share that more outwardly too, where like, what benefits are they getting?
So it's, that's kind of like solving more of like the discovery piece and that gives you like volume.
And then I think the other piece is like the depth piece where it's like, yes, they're invested.
Now they want to pay for the community.
I think that's another like problem that, you know, it's a problem to solve where it's like, yeah, there's people who are consuming content for free.
Like all of these people who are reading the content, but like, how do we get them into a paid member?
I think that's where like, you know, think of it like a product, like what extra features would make it really attractive to those people.
Have done this at Mutiny is like for the earlier members, literally just asking them and like being very transparent with like, hey, we want to really take this community to the next level.

Yeah, so usually we have like a speaker who we either source from like our current customer base because we like to find someone who knows how to use the product or at least is like a, you know, somewhat of an influencer in the topic.
So we work on figuring out like a brief with them on what the topic should be.
And then we have like a template that I usually just send them where they have to like fill out where it's like, what are the three main takeaways you want the attendees to get from this event?
What is the deliverable you want to get?
Because we want this to be very tactical, right?
Like people should be working on something.
So the deliverable could either be like being able to build a workflow in Apollo, like start to end using the sequences function, like that could be a deliverable.
Some people, they don't use that kind of deliverable.
They might say like filling out an ICP worksheet and being able to be confident in applying that to your workflow, like that could be the deliverable.
And then afterwards, you know, we host everything on Luma, like in terms of the landing page.

And then we also have incentive through the community as well, where it's like if you join, we either like sometimes it's like we'll give you credits for Apollo if it's like product related so that they join.
